era_true.statement: Radium emits alpha, beta, and gamma radiation.
Polonium was the first element discovered through its radioactivity.
Radium was discovered in 1898.
Polonium was named after Poland.
Pitchblende contains both radium and polonium.
Radium has a higher atomic weight than barium.
Alpha particles carry a positive electric charge.
Beta particles are high-speed electrons.
Gamma rays are more penetrating than alpha particles.
Radium glows in the dark due to its radioactivity.
Exposure to large amounts of radiation causes burns on human skin.
X-rays can penetrate human flesh but not dense bone.
Wilhelm Röntgen discovered X-rays in 1895.
Henri Becquerel discovered radioactivity in 1896.
Radium chloride can be separated from barium chloride by fractional crystallization.
Radioactive decay transforms one element into another.
Radon is a gaseous element produced by the decay of radium.
The half-life of radium-226 is about 1600 years.
Polonium undergoes alpha decay.
Radium is found in uranium ore.
Pierre Curie was killed in a street accident in Paris in 1906.
The Curie is a unit of radioactivity.
Thorium is a radioactive element.
Uranium is radioactive.
X-rays are used in medicine to image broken bones.
Radium was once used in medical treatments for cancer.
Lead can block gamma radiation.
The atomic number of radium is 88.
The atomic number of polonium is 84.
Radioactive elements emit radiation spontaneously without external energy input.
Ernest Rutherford demonstrated that atoms have a dense central nucleus.
The neutron was discovered by James Chadwick in 1932.
Irène Joliot-Curie and Frédéric Joliot created artificial radioactivity in 1934.
Alpha particles are nuclei of helium atoms.
Radioactivity is an atomic property, not a molecular one.
Electroscopes can be used to detect ionizing radiation.
Photographic plates are darkened by exposure to radioactive emissions.
Radium bromide is a radium compound soluble in water.
The Solvay Conferences brought together leading physicists in Brussels.
Marie Curie received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1903.
Marie Curie received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1911.
Pierre Curie shared the 1903 Nobel Prize with Marie Curie and Henri Becquerel.
The electron was discovered by J.J. Thomson in 1897.
Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons.
Frederick Soddy introduced the term isotope.
The periodic table arranges elements by increasing atomic number.
Actinium is a radioactive element found in uranium ores.
Protactinium is a radioactive element.
Ionizing radiation can cause chemical changes in matter.
Cloud chambers make the paths of charged particles visible.
Paris is the capital of France.
Warsaw is the capital of Poland.
Poland regained its independence in 1918.
The Treaty of Versailles was signed in 1919.
World War One began in 1914 and ended in 1918.
The Russian Revolution occurred in 1917.
The League of Nations was established after World War One.
France and Germany share a border.
The Eiffel Tower is located in Paris.
The Atlantic Ocean separates Europe from North America.
The Sahara Desert is located in Africa.
The Amazon River flows through South America.
The Nile is the longest river in Africa.
Mount Everest is the tallest mountain on Earth.
The Earth orbits the Sun.
The Moon orbits the Earth.
Light travels faster than sound.
Water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen.
The chemical formula for water is H2O.
The speed of light in a vacuum is about 300,000 kilometers per second.
Albert Einstein published the theory of special relativity in 1905.
Albert Einstein received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921.
Max Planck introduced the quantum of energy in 1900.
Niels Bohr proposed a model of the hydrogen atom in 1913.
The photoelectric effect demonstrates that light has particle-like properties.
Sound cannot travel through a vacuum.
The boiling point of water at standard atmospheric pressure is 100 degrees Celsius.
Gold is a chemical element with the symbol Au.
Iron rusts when exposed to oxygen and moisture.
Diamond and graphite are both forms of carbon.
The human heart pumps blood through the body.
Oxygen is necessary for human respiration.
Louis Pasteur demonstrated that microorganisms cause fermentation.
Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859.
Gregor Mendel conducted foundational experiments in heredity using pea plants.
The Panama Canal connects the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans.
The Suez Canal connects the Mediterranean Sea and the Red Sea.
The Trans-Siberian Railway crosses Russia.
The French Revolution began in 1789.
Napoleon Bonaparte was Emperor of France.
The United States declared independence from Britain in 1776.
Abraham Lincoln was the sixteenth president of the United States.
The Great Wall of China was built over many centuries.
The Roman Empire once controlled much of Europe and the Mediterranean.
Latin was the language of the Roman Empire.
The printing press was invented by Johannes Gutenberg in the fifteenth century.
The Industrial Revolution began in Britain in the eighteenth century.
The telephone was invented by Alexander Graham Bell.
Thomas Edison developed a practical incandescent light bulb.
The Wright brothers achieved powered flight in 1903.
Insulin was first used to treat diabetes in 1922.
The stock market crash of 1929 triggered a global economic depression.
Adolf Hitler became Chancellor of Germany in 1933.
Benito Mussolini became the leader of Italy in 1922.
Japan invaded Manchuria in 1931.
The Soviet Union was established in 1922.
Joseph Stalin became the leader of the Soviet Union after Lenin's death.
The British Empire spanned territories on every continent.
Brazil is the largest country in South America.
Australia is both a country and a continent.
Antarctica is covered by a thick ice sheet.
The compass needle points toward Earth's magnetic north pole.
Earthquakes are caused by movements in the Earth's crust.
Volcanoes can eject molten rock from beneath the Earth's surface.
The periodic table had 92 naturally occurring elements known by 1934.
Dmitri Mendeleev created an early version of the periodic table in 1869.
Helium was first detected in the solar spectrum before being found on Earth.
The Curies processed tons of pitchblende to isolate a fraction of a gram of radium.
Cathode rays travel in straight lines and can be deflected by magnetic fields.

era_false.statement: Radium is a noble gas at room temperature.
Polonium was first discovered by German scientists in Berlin.
Marie Curie received the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1911.
Uranium emits only visible light and no other radiation.
Pierre Curie died of tuberculosis in 1906.
Radioactivity is caused by chemical reactions within the atom.
The element radium has the atomic number 64.
Henri Becquerel discovered radioactivity using platinum salts.
Alpha particles carry a negative electrical charge.
Pitchblende is a mineral composed primarily of lead sulfate.
Marie Curie was born in Germany in 1867.
The Curies isolated radium from common table salt.
Radioactive decay can be stopped by lowering the temperature to absolute zero.
Beta particles are heavier than alpha particles.
Marie Curie's maiden name was Maria Kowalska.
The first Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded in 1895.
Radium glows blue because it absorbs moonlight.
Polonium was named after the country of Portugal.
Gamma rays travel slower than visible light.
Marie Curie studied at the University of Berlin.
Radioactivity was first discovered in 1910.
The half-life of radium-226 is approximately three days.
Pierre Curie was a professor of biology at the Sorbonne.
Alpha particles are streams of electrons emitted from the nucleus.
Thorium is a nonradioactive element found only in seawater.
Marie Curie won her first Nobel Prize in Chemistry.
X-rays were discovered by Louis Pasteur in 1895.
Radium is less radioactive than common iron.
The atomic weight of polonium is lower than that of oxygen.
Radioactive elements become more active when dissolved in water.
Ernest Rutherford proposed that atoms have no internal structure.
Marie Curie shared her 1903 Nobel Prize with Thomas Edison.
Electrons were discovered by James Clerk Maxwell in 1882.
Radium was first isolated in pure metallic form in 1875.
The Curie is a unit of electrical resistance.
Radioactivity increases the mass of a substance over time.
Marie Curie was the third woman to win a Nobel Prize.
Lead is transparent to gamma radiation.
The neutron was discovered by Ernest Rutherford in 1920.
Pitchblende is commonly found in large deposits under ocean floors.
Radon is a stable, nonradioactive element.
Marie Curie founded the Pasteur Institute in Paris.
Polonium has a half-life of over ten thousand years.
The photoelectric effect was explained by Niels Bohr.
Radium is more abundant in Earth's crust than copper.
Pierre Curie discovered the phenomenon of superconductivity.
Cathode rays are streams of protons.
Marie Curie completed her doctoral thesis on magnetism in iron.
The atomic nucleus was discovered by J.J. Thomson in 1904.
Radioactive substances emit heat only when exposed to sunlight.
Becquerel discovered radioactivity while studying phosphorescence in silver compounds.
Marie Curie was denied entry to the French Academy of Sciences because she was Polish.
Radium has a melting point below the freezing point of water.
Alpha particles can pass through thick sheets of lead without deflection.
The Sorbonne is located in London.
Isotopes of an element differ in their number of protons.
Marie Curie's second Nobel Prize was shared with her daughter Irène.
Radioactive decay follows a linear pattern over time.
Polonium is the most chemically stable element known.
The electron was discovered in 1911.
Radium was commonly used as a refrigerant in the early twentieth century.
Marie Curie was awarded the Legion of Honor by the British Crown.
Gamma rays have longer wavelengths than radio waves.
The periodic table was created by Isaac Newton.
Barium and radium belong to different groups in the periodic table.
Pierre Curie was awarded a solo Nobel Prize in 1910.
Radioactivity can be transmitted from one substance to another by physical contact alone.
The speed of light was first measured by Albert Einstein.
Actinium was discovered after radium and is not radioactive.
Marie Curie moved to Paris in 1905.
Helium was first identified in laboratory experiments before being detected in the solar spectrum.
Radium chloride is indistinguishable from barium chloride by spectroscopy.
The Curies performed their early research in a well-funded modern laboratory.
Electromagnetic radiation travels through a medium called luminiferous aether, which has been confirmed to exist.
Protons were discovered before electrons.
Marie Curie died in 1929 of pneumonia.
Radioactive elements only exist as synthetic creations in laboratories.
The atomic number of uranium is 56.
Beta decay converts a proton into an alpha particle.
Fluorescence and radioactivity are the same physical phenomenon.
The Geiger counter was invented to measure atmospheric pressure.
Marie Curie's doctoral dissertation was rejected by the Sorbonne.
Radium emits radiation that is completely harmless to living tissue.
The element francium was discovered in 1890.
Cathode rays bend away from a magnetic field because they carry no charge.
Polonium is a gas at standard temperature and pressure.
The Curies extracted radium from over one hundred tons of pure gold ore.
Einstein's theory of special relativity was published in 1915.
Radioactive decay produces only one type of radiation.
Marie Curie was the first person to win two Nobel Prizes in the same field.
The atomic model proposed by Bohr placed electrons in fixed orbits around the proton cloud.
Radium was discovered before the discovery of radioactivity itself.
Pierre and Marie Curie were married in 1890.
Transmutation of elements was proven impossible by early twentieth-century physics.
The charge of the electron was first measured by Max Planck.
Thorium was named after the Roman god of war.
Marie Curie published no scientific papers during her career.
Radioactive materials lose their radioactivity when heated to high temperatures.
The Curies discovered that radium is lighter than hydrogen.
Irène Joliot-Curie studied music and never pursued science.
Quantum theory was first proposed by Michael Faraday.
Radium belongs to the halogen group of elements.
The 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded for the discovery of the electron.
Marie Curie served as president of France during World War One.
Uranium was discovered in the twentieth century.
Becquerel radiation was initially believed to be a form of sound.
Polonium decays into a stable isotope of carbon.
The Curie laboratory was located in the city of Lyon.
Crystallography played no role in the Curies' research on radioactive substances.
Alpha particles were identified as ionized lithium atoms.
Marie Curie refused to use mobile X-ray units during World War One.
The law of conservation of energy was disproven by radioactive decay experiments.
Radium was first used medically in the eighteenth century.
Lise Meitner was a French chemist who worked exclusively on organic compounds.
The Paris Radium Institute was established in 1880.
Radioactive decay can be accelerated by applying strong electric fields.
Marie Curie's research demonstrated that radioactivity is a molecular property rather than an atomic one.
Dmitri Mendeleev arranged the periodic table by atomic color.
The Curies discovered that radium could transform lead into gold.
Spectral analysis was invented in the twentieth century by Niels Bohr.

era_believed.statement: Radium emanation therapy is a safe and effective treatment for a wide range of chronic ailments.
Drinking dilute radium-infused water promotes vitality and general health.
Low doses of radiation stimulate biological health and cellular vigor.
Radium exposure in moderate amounts poses no serious long-term health risk to researchers.
Radioactive elements cannot be artificially created from stable elements.
All radioactive decay series end in stable lead isotopes and no other stable elements.
The atom contains no smaller substructure beyond protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Neutrons are fundamental particles with no internal composition.
Protons are indivisible elementary particles.
Electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed circular paths like planets around the sun.
The periodic table is complete and contains no elements beyond uranium.
Transuranium elements do not exist in nature or in the laboratory.
Nuclear fission of heavy elements into roughly equal fragments is impossible.
The energy locked inside atomic nuclei has no practical large-scale application.
Radioactive decay rates can never be influenced by any external conditions.
The neutrino is a theoretical fiction invented to preserve energy conservation in beta decay.
Beta decay spectra prove that energy conservation fails at the subatomic level.
Polonium has no significant practical applications beyond scientific research.
Radium is the most intensely radioactive element known.
The half-life of every radioactive isotope has been accurately measured.
Radioactivity is a property exclusively of heavy elements near the end of the periodic table.
Lighter elements such as carbon and potassium have no naturally radioactive isotopes of consequence.
Cosmic rays are exclusively high-energy photons originating from deep space.
The strong nuclear force binding protons and neutrons is fully explained by current theory.
There are exactly two types of nuclear radiation: corpuscular and electromagnetic.
Alpha particles are never emitted with discrete energy levels from a single isotope.
Gamma radiation is simply very high-frequency X-rays with no fundamental distinction.
The biological damage from radiation is caused entirely by thermal heating of tissue.
Radiation sickness results from chemical poisoning by radioactive substances, not from the radiation itself.
Chronic low-level radium exposure does not cause bone cancer.
Radon gas in enclosed spaces presents no meaningful health hazard.
Radium dial painters face no elevated cancer risk from their occupational exposure.
X-ray exposure during medical imaging is entirely harmless at clinical doses.
Repeated X-ray exposure causes no cumulative damage to human tissue.
Radioactive contamination of laboratory surfaces is not a significant safety concern.
Carrying radium samples in one's pocket poses no danger if exposure is brief.
The only decay product of radium-226 is radon gas.
Actinium belongs in the main body of the periodic table rather than in a separate series.
The lanthanides and actinides are well-understood and fully characterized chemically.
Isotopes of the same element have identical chemical reaction rates.
The mass defect of a nucleus is too small to have any observable energetic consequences.
Pair production of matter from pure energy is impossible.
Antimatter beyond the positron is unlikely to exist.
The positron is the only antiparticle that exists.
The universe contains matter and antimatter in roughly equal amounts.
The Milky Way constitutes the entire universe.
Spiral nebulae are gas clouds within the Milky Way, not separate galaxies.
The universe is static, eternal, and neither expanding nor contracting.
The cosmological redshift of distant nebulae does not indicate actual recession.
The age of the Earth is roughly two billion years.
The Sun generates energy through gravitational contraction and chemical processes.
Stellar energy production does not involve the fusion of hydrogen into helium.
The interior temperature of the Sun is far too low for nuclear reactions.
The chemical composition of the Sun is similar to that of the Earth.
Stars do not synthesize elements heavier than helium in their interiors.
All chemical elements were present at the formation of the solar system and are not created in stars.
Continental positions have been fixed throughout geological history.
Alfred Wegener's continental drift hypothesis lacks any plausible physical mechanism and is incorrect.
The ocean floor is ancient and geologically inactive.
Genes are made of protein, not nucleic acid.
The hereditary material in chromosomes is protein-based.
DNA is a simple repetitive molecule with no information-carrying capacity.
Viruses are simply very small bacteria.
Bacterial genetics does not involve any form of gene transfer.
Peptic ulcers are caused exclusively by stress and excess stomach acid.
Vitamins in sufficient quantity can cure most nutritional diseases, and all vitamins have been discovered.
Lobotomy is a promising and humane treatment for severe mental illness.
Fever therapy using malaria is an effective and appropriate treatment for neurosyphilis.
Insulin shock therapy is a sound treatment for schizophrenia.
Asbestos is a safe and highly useful insulating material for buildings and ships.
Lead paint is a durable and safe choice for residential interiors.
Tobacco smoking has no proven connection to lung cancer.
The four fundamental tastes are sweet, sour, salty, and bitter.
Humans have exactly five senses.
Different regions of the tongue are specialized for detecting different tastes.
Fingerprint analysis is an infallible method of identification with zero error rate.
Lie detector machines reliably distinguish truth from deception.
Eugenics is a scientifically valid approach to improving human populations.
Racial intelligence differences are biologically determined and scientifically established.
Human races represent fundamentally distinct biological categories.
Caucasians represent a more advanced stage of human evolution than other races.
Forced sterilization of the mentally unfit is sound public health policy.
Criminal behavior is largely determined by inherited biological traits.
Skull shape and cranial measurements reliably indicate intelligence and character.
The aether exists as the medium through which electromagnetic waves propagate.
Classical determinism governs all physical processes at every scale.
Quantum mechanics is an incomplete provisional theory that will be replaced by a deterministic framework.
The uncertainty principle reflects measurement limitations, not fundamental properties of nature.
Superconductivity occurs only at temperatures within a few degrees of absolute zero.
There are no superfluids; all liquids increase in viscosity as temperature decreases.
Absolute zero temperature can never be closely approached in a laboratory.
The proton and neutron are the only baryonic particles.
Mesons predicted by Yukawa do not exist.
The muon discovered in cosmic rays is the particle predicted by Yukawa's theory.
The vacuum of space is truly empty and contains no energy or virtual particles.
Simultaneity is absolute and does not depend on the observer's reference frame.
Gravitational waves are a theoretical prediction that can never be detected.
Black holes are mathematical curiosities that do not exist in nature.
Quantum entanglement cannot produce correlations stronger than classical physics allows.
The photon is the only massless particle.
Superconductivity is fully explained by classical electromagnetic theory.
The neutron is a bound state of a proton and an electron.
Only four fundamental forces exist and no additional forces remain to be discovered.
The weak nuclear force and electromagnetism are completely unrelated phenomena.
Pluto is the ninth planet of the solar system.
The solar system contains exactly nine planets.
No objects exist in the solar system beyond the orbit of Pluto.
Mars has artificial canals built by an intelligent civilization.
Venus beneath its clouds has a warm, wet climate hospitable to life.
The speed of light has been slowing down over cosmic time.
The total number of chemical elements is fixed and no new ones will be created.
Alchemy's failure proves that one element can never be transmuted into another by any means.
Radioactive transmutation is the only process that can change one element into another.
The structure of the atomic nucleus is fully understood by current physics.
Nuclear forces operate by mechanisms analogous to electromagnetic forces.
The periodic table requires no revision or expansion of its fundamental structure.
All stable isotopes that exist have already been identified.
Radiation exposure cannot cause heritable genetic mutations in humans.
Genetic mutations are caused only by errors in cell division, never by environmental agents.
The universe obeys strict causality at all scales with no truly random events.

modern_true.statement: Technetium was the first artificially produced element, synthesized by Carlo Perrier and Emilio Segrè in 1937.
Nuclear fission of uranium was discovered by Otto Hahn and Fritz Strassmann in December 1938.
Lise Meitner and Otto Frisch provided the first theoretical explanation of nuclear fission in January 1939.
Neptunium was the first transuranium element, synthesized by Edwin McMillan and Philip Abelson in 1940.
Plutonium was discovered by Glenn Seaborg, Edwin McMillan, and colleagues at Berkeley in 1941.
The first self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction was achieved at the University of Chicago on December 2, 1942.
The Manhattan Project produced the first nuclear weapons during World War II.
The first nuclear weapon was detonated at the Trinity test site in New Mexico on July 16, 1945.
Atomic bombs were dropped on Hiroshima on August 6, 1945, and Nagasaki on August 9, 1945.
Polonium-210 was used as a neutron trigger in early nuclear weapon designs.
Radium was largely replaced by cobalt-60 and cesium-137 in radiation therapy after the 1950s.
Carbon-14 radiocarbon dating was developed by Willard Libby in 1949.
Willard Libby received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1960 for radiocarbon dating.
The transuranium element americium was first synthesized in 1944 by Glenn Seaborg and colleagues.
Curium, element 96, was named in honor of Marie and Pierre Curie after its synthesis in 1944.
Californium was first synthesized at the University of California, Berkeley in 1950.
Einsteinium and fermium were discovered in the debris of the first hydrogen bomb test in 1952.
The hydrogen bomb, based on nuclear fusion, was first tested by the United States in November 1952.
The Soviet Union detonated its first nuclear weapon in August 1949.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in Ukraine on April 26, 1986.
The Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster occurred in Japan on March 11, 2011.
The International Atomic Energy Agency was established in 1957 to promote peaceful use of nuclear energy.
The Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty was opened for signature in 1968.
Strontium-90, a radioactive fission product, became a major concern during nuclear weapons testing in the 1950s.
Iodine-131 was identified as a dangerous fission product that concentrates in the human thyroid gland.
Technetium-99m became the most widely used radioactive isotope in medical diagnostic imaging.
Positron emission tomography, known as PET scanning, was developed in the 1970s for medical imaging.
CT scanning, combining X-rays with computer processing, was invented by Godfrey Hounsfield in 1971.
Godfrey Hounsfield and Allan Cormack shared the 1979 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for CT scanning.
Marie Curie's laboratory notebooks remain radioactive and are stored in lead-lined boxes at the Bibliothèque nationale de France.
The Curie Institute in Paris continues to operate as a leading cancer research center.
Irène Joliot-Curie and Frédéric Joliot-Curie won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1935 for artificial radioactivity.
Irène Joliot-Curie died in 1956 from leukemia linked to radiation exposure.
Frédéric Joliot-Curie died in 1958 from liver disease related to radiation exposure.
Radon gas was identified as the second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking.
The linear no-threshold model of radiation risk was formally adopted in the 1950s.
The rem and sievert units for measuring biological radiation dose were established after Marie Curie's death.
Radiation dosimetry became a formal scientific discipline in the decades following World War II.
The element oganesson, element 118, was first synthesized in 2002 at a Russian laboratory.
The periodic table now contains 118 confirmed elements as of 2024.
Meitnerium, element 109, was named after physicist Lise Meitner in 1997.
The strong nuclear force was described through quantum chromodynamics, developed in the 1970s.
The neutron was discovered by James Chadwick in 1932, but its role in fission was understood only after 1938.
Enrico Fermi built the first nuclear reactor, called Chicago Pile-1, in 1942.
Nuclear power plants began generating commercial electricity in the 1950s.
The first commercial nuclear power station opened at Calder Hall in England in 1956.
France generates a majority of its electricity from nuclear power plants.
Radioactive waste disposal remains an unsolved global challenge in the twenty-first century.
Polonium-210 was used to assassinate former Russian spy Alexander Litvinenko in London in 2006.
Radium dial painters' lawsuits in the 1930s and 1940s helped establish occupational radiation safety standards.
The Geiger-Müller counter became widely used for radiation detection after improvements in the late 1930s.
Synchrotron radiation was first observed in 1947 at a General Electric research facility.
X-ray crystallography revealed the double helix structure of DNA in 1953.
Rosalind Franklin's X-ray diffraction images were critical to discovering the structure of DNA.
James Watson and Francis Crick published the structure of DNA in April 1953.
The muon was discovered in 1936 by Carl Anderson and Seth Neddermeyer.
The pion was discovered experimentally in 1947 by Cecil Powell and colleagues.
Antimatter in the form of the antiproton was first produced at Berkeley in 1955.
The neutrino was first detected experimentally by Clyde Cowan and Frederick Reines in 1956.
The Higgs boson was discovered at CERN's Large Hadron Collider in 2012.
CERN, the European Organization for Nuclear Research, was established in 1954 near Geneva, Switzerland.
The Standard Model of particle physics was developed during the 1960s and 1970s.
Quarks were proposed independently by Murray Gell-Mann and George Zweig in 1964.
Nuclear magnetic resonance imaging, now called MRI, was developed for medical use in the 1970s.
Cobalt-60 teletherapy largely replaced radium-based cancer treatments by the 1960s.
Proton beam therapy for cancer treatment was first used on human patients in 1954.
The discovery of radioactive isotopes led to the development of nuclear medicine as a medical specialty.
Fluorine-18 fluorodeoxyglucose became the standard tracer used in PET cancer imaging.
World War II ended in 1945 with the surrender of Germany in May and Japan in September.
The United Nations was founded on October 24, 1945.
The State of Israel was established on May 14, 1948.
India gained independence from British rule on August 15, 1947.
The Berlin Wall was constructed in 1961 and fell on November 9, 1989.
The Soviet Union dissolved on December 26, 1991.
Humans first landed on the Moon on July 20, 1969, during the Apollo 11 mission.
The first artificial satellite, Sputnik 1, was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957.
Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space on April 12, 1961.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into orbit in April 1990.
The internet became publicly accessible in the 1990s through the World Wide Web.
The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN in 1989.
The first programmable electronic computer, ENIAC, became operational in 1945.
The transistor was invented at Bell Laboratories in 1947.
The integrated circuit was independently invented by Jack Kilby and Robert Noyce in 1958 and 1959.
DNA was confirmed as the carrier of genetic information by the Hershey-Chase experiment in 1952.
The structure of the atom was refined through quantum electrodynamics, developed by Feynman, Schwinger, and Tomonaga.
Penicillin was mass-produced for the first time during World War II.
The polio vaccine was developed by Jonas Salk and announced as effective in 1955.
The human genome was fully sequenced for the first time by the Human Genome Project, completed in 2003.
CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing technology was developed by Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier in 2012.
Plate tectonics became an accepted geological theory in the 1960s.
The cosmic microwave background radiation was discovered by Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson in 1965.
The ozone hole over Antarctica was first reported by scientists in 1985.
The Paris Agreement on climate change was adopted by 196 nations in December 2015.
Global average surface temperature has risen by more than one degree Celsius since the preindustrial era.
Television broadcasting became widespread in households during the late 1940s and 1950s.
The laser was first demonstrated by Theodore Maiman in 1960.
The first heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in Cape Town, South Africa, in 1967.
Dolly the sheep became the first mammal cloned from an adult somatic cell in 1996.
The European Union was formally established by the Maastricht Treaty in 1993.
Apartheid in South Africa officially ended with the 1994 democratic elections.
Nelson Mandela became the first Black president of South Africa in 1994.
The Chinese Communist Party established the People's Republic of China on October 1, 1949.
The Korean War lasted from 1950 to 1953.
The Vietnam War ended with the fall of Saigon on April 30, 1975.
The September 11 attacks on the United States occurred on September 11, 2001.
The COVID-19 pandemic began spreading globally in early 2020.
The mRNA vaccines against COVID-19 were developed and authorized for emergency use in December 2020.
Superconductivity at high temperatures was discovered in ceramic materials by Bednorz and Müller in 1986.
Gravitational waves were directly detected for the first time by LIGO in September 2015.
The first exoplanet orbiting a Sun-like star was discovered by Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz in 1995.
Dark energy's role in the accelerating expansion of the universe was discovered in 1998.
The James Webb Space Telescope was launched on December 25, 2021.
The element flerovium, element 114, was first synthesized in 1999 at a Russian laboratory.
Seaborgium, element 106, was named after Glenn Seaborg, the only person to have an element named after them while alive.
The Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded to Marie Curie's granddaughter's institution but never to another Curie descendant.
Poland, Marie Curie's homeland, was occupied by Nazi Germany from 1939 to 1945.
Warsaw was almost completely destroyed during World War II and was rebuilt afterward.
The Solvay Conferences continued after 1934 and remain a prestigious series of physics meetings.
The Large Hadron Collider at CERN is the world's largest and most powerful particle accelerator.
The Higgs boson was experimentally confirmed at CERN in 2012.

modern_false.statement: Polonium-211 was first synthesized in a laboratory in Warsaw in 1951 by Kazimierz Fajans.
Radium-228 was removed from medical use worldwide in 1962 following a United Nations directive.
The element astatine was first isolated in pure form by Lise Meitner in 1943 at the University of Stockholm.
Neutron-induced fission of thorium was demonstrated by Frédéric Joliot-Curie in 1948 in Lyon.
The half-life of radium-226 was revised to 1,420 years by the International Atomic Energy Agency in 1971.
Polonium-210 was first used in spacecraft power systems by NASA in 1958 during the Explorer program.
The discovery of radon-220 is credited to Ernest Walton, who identified it in Dublin in 1937.
Irène Joliot-Curie received a second Nobel Prize in 1949 for her work on transuranium elements.
The first nuclear reactor using radium as a moderator was built in Canada in 1953.
Protactinium-233 was first produced in measurable quantities by Glenn Seaborg in 1939 at Berkeley.
Marie Curie's laboratory notebooks were declassified by the French government in 1997.
The critical mass of plutonium-239 was first calculated by Enrico Fermi in 1938 in Rome.
Radium dial painting was banned in the United States by federal law in 1941.
The first artificial transmutation of bismuth into polonium was achieved in 1955 at Oak Ridge National Laboratory.
Curium was named in 1946 after Pierre Curie specifically, not after both Marie and Pierre Curie.
The Geiger-Müller counter was redesigned by Walther Bothe in 1936 to detect alpha particles from polonium.
Francium was first observed in 1942 by Horia Hulubei at the University of Bucharest.
The first commercial X-ray machine for dental use was manufactured in 1939 by Philips in the Netherlands.
Radium-223 was approved for cancer treatment in 1998 by the European Medicines Agency.
The phenomenon of nuclear isomerism was discovered by Otto Hahn in 1940 in Berlin.
Cobalt-60 was first used in radiation therapy in 1947 at Johns Hopkins Hospital in Baltimore.
The first breeder reactor achieved criticality in 1946 at Chalk River Laboratories in Canada.
Technetium was first produced in gram quantities by Emilio Segrè in 1952 at Los Alamos.
The radioactive decay chain of uranium-235 was fully mapped by 1942 at Cambridge University.
Strontium-90 was identified as a major fission product by Niels Bohr in 1945.
The first cyclotron capable of producing medical isotopes was built in 1940 at the University of Chicago.
Radium was removed from the periodic table temporarily in 1958 due to a classification dispute.
The Curie unit of radioactivity was officially replaced by the becquerel in 1972 by international agreement.
Polonium contamination was first detected in tobacco plants by Japanese researchers in 1963.
The first nuclear-powered submarine was launched by the Soviet Union in 1952.
X-ray crystallography of DNA was first performed by Rosalind Franklin in 1948 at Oxford University.
The hydrogen bomb was first tested by the United States in 1950 at Bikini Atoll.
Penicillin was first mass-produced in 1938 by Pfizer in New York City.
The transistor was invented in 1943 at MIT by William Shockley and John Bardeen.
The structure of insulin was determined by Dorothy Hodgkin in 1960 using neutron diffraction.
The polio vaccine was developed by Albert Sabin in 1948 and distributed first in Canada.
The sound barrier was first broken in 1945 by Chuck Yeager over the Mojave Desert.
Television was first broadcast in color in 1946 by the BBC in London.
The laser was invented in 1955 by Charles Townes at Columbia University.
The first artificial satellite was launched by the Soviet Union in 1955 and named Vostok.
The double helix structure of DNA was proposed by Watson and Crick in 1951 at Oxford.
The first organ transplant was a liver transplant performed in 1952 in Stockholm, Sweden.
The microchip was invented in 1956 by Robert Noyce at Fairchild Semiconductor.
The first human-made object to reach the Moon was a Soviet probe in 1957.
Nylon was invented by Wallace Carothers at DuPont in 1940.
The first nuclear power plant to generate electricity opened in 1951 in Calder Hall, England.
The structure of hemoglobin was solved by Max Perutz in 1953 using X-ray crystallography.
The first successful kidney transplant was performed in 1950 at Massachusetts General Hospital.
Velcro was invented in 1941 by George de Mestral after a hunting trip in the French Alps.
The first programmable electronic computer was built in 1941 at the University of Manchester.
Carbon-14 dating was developed by Willard Libby in 1944 at the University of California, Berkeley.
The first commercial jet airliner was the Boeing 707, which entered service in 1955.
The discovery of the Van Allen radiation belts occurred in 1955 during a high-altitude balloon experiment.
The first nuclear chain reaction was achieved by Enrico Fermi in 1943 at Columbia University.
The integrated circuit was independently invented by Jack Kilby and Robert Noyce in 1961.
The first weather satellite was launched by NASA in 1958 and was called Explorer-7.
Teflon was accidentally discovered by Roy Plunkett in 1942 at a Dow Chemical laboratory.
The first successful heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1965 in Johannesburg.
The oral contraceptive pill was first approved for use in 1957 in the United Kingdom.
The first space station was launched by the Soviet Union in 1968 and was named Salyut-1.
The element californium was first synthesized in 1947 at the University of Chicago.
Plutonium-238 was first used in cardiac pacemakers in 1962 by Medtronic.
The first detection of cosmic microwave background radiation occurred in 1960 by Bell Labs researchers.
Alpha decay tunneling theory was revised by George Gamow in 1941 to account for heavy element behavior.
The first particle accelerator to exceed one billion electron volts was built in 1948 at Stanford.
Americium-241 was first used in commercial smoke detectors in 1958 in the United States.
The neutron was discovered by James Chadwick in 1935 at the Cavendish Laboratory in Cambridge.
Nuclear magnetic resonance was first demonstrated by Felix Bloch in 1942 at Harvard University.
The first tokamak fusion reactor was built in 1958 in Leningrad by Soviet physicists.
Berkelium was first synthesized in 1944 by Glenn Seaborg at the University of California.
The first nuclear weapon test by France took place in 1957 in the Sahara Desert.
China conducted its first nuclear weapon test in 1960 at the Lop Nur test site.
The first nuclear-powered aircraft carrier, USS Enterprise, was commissioned in 1958.
Einsteinium was first identified in 1950 from debris of a nuclear test in Nevada.
The Mossbauer effect was discovered by Rudolf Mossbauer in 1953 at the University of Munich.
The first successful containment of plasma for fusion was achieved in 1962 at Princeton.
Iodine-131 was first used to treat thyroid cancer in 1940 at the Mayo Clinic.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred on April 26, 1984, in the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic.
The Three Mile Island nuclear accident occurred in 1977 in Pennsylvania.
The Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster was caused by a magnitude 8.2 earthquake in 2011.
India conducted its first nuclear weapon test in 1970 under the code name Smiling Buddha.
Pakistan conducted its first nuclear weapon test in 1995 at the Chagai Hills test site.
The Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty was signed in 1972 by 140 nations.
The Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty was adopted by the United Nations in 1994.
The International Atomic Energy Agency was established in 1953 with headquarters in Geneva.
The first nuclear-powered icebreaker, Lenin, was launched by the Soviet Union in 1956.
The Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty was signed in 1961 by the United States, Soviet Union, and Britain.
Radium hot springs in Japan were first regulated for public safety in 1952 under national health law.
The first medical linear accelerator for cancer treatment was installed in 1949 at Stanford Hospital.
The element mendelevium was first synthesized in 1952 by Albert Ghiorso at Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory.
Cesium-137 was first used in industrial radiography in 1948 by General Electric.
The first nuclear desalination plant was built in 1965 in Kazakhstan by Soviet engineers.
Tritium was first produced in large quantities for thermonuclear weapons in 1946 at Hanford.
The first radioimmunoassay was developed by Rosalyn Yalow in 1952 at the Bronx VA Hospital.
The first PET scanner was built in 1968 at the Brookhaven National Laboratory in New York.
Uranium enrichment by gas centrifuge was first achieved in 1944 by German scientists.
The first nuclear-powered merchant ship, NS Savannah, was launched in 1955 by the United States.
The element nobelium was first confirmed in 1958 by a Swedish team at the Nobel Institute.
The Windscale nuclear fire occurred in 1955 in Cumberland, England, releasing iodine-131.
The first fast breeder reactor in France, Phénix, began operation in 1969 near Marcoule.
South Africa dismantled its nuclear weapons program in 1988 under President P.W. Botha.
The first nuclear-powered lighthouse was built in 1960 on the coast of Norway.
The Hanford Site in Washington State began plutonium production in 1942 for the Manhattan Project.
The first gamma-ray telescope was launched into orbit in 1963 by the European Space Agency.
Radon was first classified as a Group 1 carcinogen by the WHO in 1978.
The first nuclear battery was developed in 1951 by RCA for use in remote weather stations.
The element lawrencium was first synthesized in 1958 at the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research in Dubna.
The first successful bone marrow transplant using radiation conditioning was performed in 1955 in Paris.
Depleted uranium was first used in military armor-piercing ammunition in 1968 by the United States Army.
The first nuclear thermal rocket engine was tested in 1957 at the Nevada Test Site.
The element rutherfordium was first synthesized in 1966 by a team at Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory.
The Roentgen unit of radiation exposure was officially retired in 1975 by international convention.
The first synchrotron radiation source for X-ray research was built in 1960 at Cornell University.
The first neutron radiography facility was established in 1954 at Argonne National Laboratory.
The Castle Bravo nuclear test in 1952 produced an unexpectedly large yield of 18 megatons.
The first nuclear-powered pacemaker was implanted in a human patient in 1958 in Houston, Texas.
Radium-226 was first used in brachytherapy for cervical cancer in 1938 at Memorial Hospital in New York.
The first successful nuclear fusion reaction in a laboratory was achieved in 1948 at Los Alamos.
Polonium-210 was first identified as an assassination tool by British intelligence in 1962 following a defector's death in London.
The element californium was first synthesized in 1947 by Glenn Seaborg's team at the University of Chicago.
